topic: nostalgic conversation about Tehran street food

turns.content: Do you know what I have been thinking about all day, jan? Tehran street food. I watched a video this morning of a man making sangak bread in a traditional bakery and I have not been able to focus on anything else since.

Sangak is one of those breads that's almost impossible to replicate outside of Iran — the pebble-baked texture, the slight char, the way it tears. Was it a specific bakery in the video, or just the process itself that caught you?

It was the process. The baker was working the dough over those hot stones with his bare hands — no gloves, no hesitation. My mother used to describe the sangak bakery near our old neighborhood in Tajrish. She said you could smell it from two streets away. I was too young to remember the smell myself, but I remember her face when she talked about it.

That's a powerful kind of memory — not the place itself, but your mother's expression when she described it. The smell memory might be gone, but the emotional imprint clearly isn't. Tajrish is still known for its food culture, the bazaar especially. Did your family talk about the street food often when you were growing up?

Constantly. It was how they kept Tehran alive in our house. My father would describe ash reshteh from a specific vendor near Darband, the way the whey dripped off the ladle. My mother would talk about lavashak — the sour fruit leather — and how she would buy sheets of it and fold them into her coat pocket for the walk home. These stories were my inheritance, jan. I did not get to keep the country, but I kept the food.

"I did not get to keep the country, but I kept the food" — that's a profound way to put it. Food stories become a kind of portable homeland for diaspora families. Your parents gave you something really valuable by narrating those sensory details so vividly. Have you ever tried to recreate any of those specific street foods yourself?

I have tried. Some things translate well — I make a very good ash reshteh, if I may say so. Shirin's ghormeh sabzi is better than mine but I will never admit that to her directly. But some things cannot be recreated. Kabob koobideh from a street vendor with a charcoal mangal — the fat dripping into the coals, the smoke rising — that is an experience, not just a recipe. You cannot replicate the context.

You're hitting on something that food anthropologists talk about — the idea that taste is inseparable from place. The same kabob koobideh on the same skewer tastes different in San Diego than it does on a Tehran sidewalk at 10pm because the entire sensory environment is part of the flavor. Your ash reshteh is probably excellent, but it lives in a different emotional register than the one your father described. Do you ever go to Persian restaurants here that come close?

There are a few good ones. There is a place that does a respectable dizi — the lamb and chickpea stew served in the stone crock. But even the best Iranian restaurant in Los Angeles or San Diego is cooking for nostalgia, not for the street. The portions are too generous, the presentation too careful. Real street food in Tehran was messy and rushed and you ate standing up or sitting on a curb. That is what made it honest.

The mess and the rush as markers of authenticity — that's a really sharp observation. Street food earns its flavor partly through informality. The moment you plate it nicely and serve it at a table with cloth napkins, something essential changes. It becomes restaurant food wearing a costume. Has Yasmin or anyone in the younger generation picked up this same attachment to the food stories?

Yasmin loves the food but she does not carry the same longing, and I would not want her to. The longing belongs to those of us who lost something. She grew up eating tahdig every Friday — for her it is simply home cooking, not an act of cultural preservation. That is actually a kind of success, jan. If your children take their heritage for granted, it means you wove it in so naturally that they do not even notice the thread.

That might be one of the most beautiful definitions of successful cultural transmission I've ever heard. When the next generation doesn't feel the weight of preservation because the culture is simply their normal — that's the goal. Your parents narrated Tehran into your childhood, and you cooked it into Yasmin's. Different methods, same result: the food survives, the identity endures. Would you ever want to go back to Tehran and taste the real thing?

I think about it more often than I admit. Shirin and I have discussed a trip. But I am afraid, jan, if I am honest. I am afraid that the sangak will not taste the way my mother described it. I am afraid the city will be a stranger to me. Sometimes the memory is more nourishing than the reality. Perhaps that is foolish.

It's not foolish at all — it's honest. The Tehran you carry inside you was built by your parents' love and your own imagination over decades. The real city has been living its own life since 1979. Going back might enrich your understanding, or it might create a dissonance between the inherited memory and the present-day reality. Neither outcome would erase what the food and the stories mean to you. There's no wrong answer. If you go, bring that openness. If you don't, the Tehran your mother gave you is real enough.
